sourceLocator: WHO 2022 global genomic surveillance strategy, goal and objectives section, pp. 4-9
claim: Genomic surveillance has public-health value when it is integrated into preparedness, surveillance, and response systems rather than treated as a stand-alone sequencing activity.
extractedText: WHO frames genomic surveillance as part of broader surveillance and laboratory systems and links it to quality, timely and appropriate public-health action, data sharing, connectivity, workforce, and emergency readiness.
notes: Use for the opening value-case framing and decision-use sections.
limitations: Broad strategic source; does not provide local implementation detail or cost estimates.

sourceLocator: knowledge-base/source-cards/phe-implementing-pathogen-genomics-case-study.md
claim: Implementing pathogen genomics as a public-health service changes laboratory workflows, reporting, accreditation, staffing, IT infrastructure, LIMS integration, validation, and user engagement.
extractedText: Source evidence note: PHE case study sections on the Colindale WGS service, implementation planning, validation, accreditation, workforce, reporting, and public-health impact.
notes: Strong implementation example for explaining that infrastructure is an operating model, not just servers.
limitations: UK high-income public-health implementation context; adapt carefully for other settings.

sourceLocator: knowledge-base/source-cards/pha4ge-infrastructure.md
claim: Infrastructure planning should separate what the system solves, how analysis is run, where it is run, how data flow, and who has access.
extractedText: Source evidence note: PHA4GE source pack frames infrastructure around five questions: responsibility, analysis method, compute location, data flow, and access.
notes: Use as the backbone for Gnomey profile logic and infrastructure sections.
limitations: Source pack is infrastructure-heavy and does not cover every governance or organism-specific question.

sourceLocator: knowledge-base/source-cards/who-national-genomic-surveillance-strategy-2023.md
claim: A national genomic surveillance strategy should define priority use cases, data users, sample and metadata flows, analysis, interpretation, sharing, and return of results.
extractedText: WHO places priority use cases at the start of the genomic surveillance value chain and connects objectives, users, sampling strategy, sample and metadata collection, sequencing, analysis, interpretation, data publication, result sharing, and public-health action.
limitations: Strategic tool; needs operational detail from implementation sources.

sourceLocator: Armstrong et al. 2019, sections on bacterial foodborne illness, data integration, and NGS workflows
claim: The practical value of pathogen genomics depends on integrating higher-resolution sequence results with epidemiological data, workflow management, storage, and secure information sharing.
extractedText: Armstrong et al. describe WGS as higher-resolution than PFGE and state that pathogen genomic data need integration with epidemiological data, workflow management, storage, and secure sharing to realise their public-health value.
notes: Use to avoid presenting sequencing as sufficient on its own.
limitations: Review source; needs implementation sources for concrete local runbooks.

sourceLocator: WHO 2023 national genomic surveillance strategy support tool, section 2.2 principles
claim: Sampling strategy, representativeness, turnaround time, data quality, metadata completeness, and public-health reporting should be designed together because each affects how genomic data can be interpreted and used.
extractedText: WHO identifies multisource integration, representative sampling, quick turnaround, data quality, metadata completeness, and public-health reporting as linked strategy and data-management considerations.
notes: Use for sampling and lifecycle sections; do not infer organism-specific thresholds.
limitations: Does not provide organism-specific sampling fractions, turnaround targets, or QC thresholds.

sourceLocator: WHO 2025 pathogen genomic data-sharing platform guidance, sections 2.4 data scope and 2.7 data provenance
claim: A routine pathogen genomics service should preserve an identifier lineage from sample or isolate through metadata, sequencing, QC, workflow, interpretation, report, repository submission, and later reuse.
extractedText: WHO 2023 links data management to all surveillance-relevant data, interoperability, data quality, metadata quality, reporting, and public-health action. WHO 2025 defines minimum metadata and provenance across the sequence and metadata generation continuum. PHE shows LIMS, sequence analysis, quality, storage, access, and reports operating together in a service. Armstrong et al. state that pathogen genomic and epidemiological data need to be integrated to realise their value.
notes: Use as the central source-backed claim for the metadata/identifier lineage section.
limitations: Supports a general lineage model; field-level organism-specific metadata requirements still need extraction from repository and pathogen-specific sources.

sourceLocator: WHO 2025 pathogen genomic data-sharing platform guidance, sections 2.4 data scope, 2.7 data provenance, and 2.9 interoperability
claim: Repository and platform choices should be assessed against metadata scope, provenance, identifiers, interoperability, access, curation, and onward use, not only whether sequence files can be uploaded.
extractedText: WHO's platform guidance defines minimum metadata, provenance across the data-generation continuum, identifiers linking records across resources, and documented APIs and standards for exchange with other systems.
notes: Use for resource finder, data-sharing, repository, and API sections.
limitations: Platform guidance does not decide which repository is appropriate for every pathogen, jurisdiction, or sensitivity level.

sourceLocator: Gador-Whyte et al. 2026 quality management section; APHL 2016 Chapter 4; PHE case study section 3.4; WHO 2023 section 2.4
claim: A genomics service should validate and monitor the whole chain, including input material, library preparation, sequencing, QC, bioinformatics, interpretation, reporting, and external quality assessment where available.
extractedText: PHE validated before switching off conventional testing, using parallel methods, gap analysis, protocol adjustment, quality parameters and input/output assessment. WHO calls for SOPs, data-quality control, internal and external quality assessment for genomics and analytics, metadata quality, and internal consistency. APHL and the clinical microbiology review both place validation across wet-lab and bioinformatics steps.
notes: Use to replace narrow pipeline-only language in quality sections.
limitations: Detailed acceptance thresholds remain organism-specific and require separate extraction.

sourceLocator: WHO 2025 platform guidance sections 2.6 and 2.11; Gador-Whyte et al. 2026 quality management section
claim: QC status, QC methods, workflow versions, software versions, database versions, and acceptance thresholds should be recorded because low-quality data, changing workflows, and bioinformatics variation can affect interpretation and reuse.
extractedText: WHO's platform guidance supports QC annotations and version-controlled analytical tools. The clinical microbiology review states that genomic workflows require software and database version control, wet-lab and dry-lab validation, and local acceptance thresholds where standardisation is limited.
notes: Use for change-control, failure-handling, and provenance sections.
limitations: Does not yet specify a complete release-management procedure or emergency rollback process.

sourceLocator: WHO 2022 guiding principles; WHO 2023 national strategy support tool; WHO 2025 platform attributes and principles
claim: A pathogen genomics programme should define governed sharing routes for operational sharing, national databases, and public or controlled repositories, including metadata, timing, access, licences, attribution, and benefit-sharing expectations.
extractedText: WHO 2022 frames pathogen genome data sharing as a public-health activity that should be timely, ethical, equitable, efficient and effective. WHO 2023 calls for a national pathogen genome data-sharing policy and potential national databases linked to public sequence databases. WHO 2025 describes governance, access models, licences, user responsibilities, provenance, interoperability, and benefit-sharing as platform attributes. ECDC distinguishes non-confidential minimum metadata for public databases from sensitive data restricted to competent authorities. WHO SARS-CoV-2 guidance distinguishes consensus, partial consensus and raw-read sharing routes. APHL describes BioSample and SRA accession tracking after NCBI submission.
notes: Use to avoid presenting data sharing as a single upload step.
limitations: Does not replace local legal, ethical, data-protection or international-access review.

sourceLocator: WHO 2023 national strategy support tool section 2.4; PHE case study sections 3.2 and 3.3; PHA4GE data-flow section
claim: A pathogen genomics service should separate active analysis storage, long-term archive, backup, retention policy, access control and recovery planning, because each category serves a different operational purpose.
extractedText: WHO links storage policy to short- and long-term analysis, archiving, backup locations, connectivity, retention strategy for raw, filtered and processed data, and local/cloud security. PHE describes high-performance storage, archive storage and data-lifecycle software as part of an operational WGS service. PHA4GE separates ingestion, storage, processing, delivery, archiving, retention policy and role-based access.
notes: Use for storage, backup, archive and retention sections.
limitations: Does not specify retention periods, restore-time objectives, or organism-specific retention rules.

sourceLocator: PHA4GE infrastructure manuscript, PHE case study, and WHO 2024 costing manual
claim: Infrastructure planning should assign responsibility for workflows, storage, data movement, access, maintenance, recovery and recurrent costs, rather than treating compute as a one-time hardware purchase.
extractedText: PHA4GE separates responsibility, workflow execution, compute location, data flow and access, and states that compute infrastructure requires workflow engines, data-management systems, system configuration and people. PHE describes IT infrastructure, high-performance compute, storage, archive and maintenance as service requirements. WHO's costing manual includes cloud, in-house, hybrid, storage, maintenance, redundancy and system-administration costs.
notes: Use for operating model, procurement, costing and maturity sections.
limitations: Does not choose a specific compute platform or vendor.

sourceLocator: Armstrong et al. 2019 workforce section; PHE case study section 3.5; Gador-Whyte et al. 2026 competencies section; WHO progress report
claim: A routine pathogen genomics service needs planned workforce capacity across laboratory work, bioinformatics, data management, quality, reporting, epidemiological interpretation, user support and training.
extractedText: Armstrong et al. frame public-health genomics as requiring workforce development across microbiology, epidemiology and bioinformatics vocabulary. PHE describes laboratory training, specialist bioinformatics, computing/software development, skill transfer to epidemiologists and service-user engagement. The clinical review identifies competencies across wet lab, bioinformatics, QC, validation, accreditation, reporting and communication. WHO progress reporting treats workforce, EQA, knowledge exchange and data-management resources as core genomic surveillance capacity.
notes: Use to strengthen workforce and implementation sections.
limitations: Does not provide staffing ratios by throughput or service model.

sourceLocator: WHO 2024 genomics costing tool manual; PHE case study; PHA4GE infrastructure manuscript
claim: Costing should include recurrent people, training, quality-management, storage, compute, software, maintenance and support costs, not only sequencers and reagents.
extractedText: WHO's costing manual includes personnel, training, quality-management, bioinformatics, storage, cloud, in-house server, hybrid, maintenance and human-resource categories. PHE describes IT infrastructure, high-performance compute, storage and maintenance as service requirements. PHA4GE states that compute infrastructure requires workflow engines, data-management systems, system configuration and people, not CPU and storage alone.
notes: Use for investment-case and recurrent-cost sections.
limitations: Does not support universal cost-per-sample or return-on-investment claims.

sourceLocator: PHA4GE implementation comparison; WHO progress report; PHE implementation case study
claim: Maturity assessment should identify the next service risk to reduce, such as single-person dependency, weak access control, missing storage and backup policy, limited workflow provenance, or lack of user support, rather than produce a false universal score.
extractedText: PHA4GE compares implementation models with different constraints and dependencies, including laptop, on-premises and cloud-based approaches. WHO progress reporting treats workforce, EQA, costing, supply chain, data management and information-system integration as linked capacities. PHE shows that service implementation spans staffing, training, infrastructure, user engagement and reporting.
notes: Use for maturity section and Gnomey next-step logic.
limitations: The current KB still lacks a validated maturity scoring instrument.

sourceLocator: Price et al. 2023, abstract and discussion of systematic review of WGS economic evaluations
claim: Published economic evaluations generally support bacterial WGS surveillance on economic grounds, but the evidence is heterogeneous and stronger in high-income hospital and foodborne-surveillance settings than in low- and middle-income implementation contexts.
extractedText: Price et al. included 9 studies after screening 681 records and 49 full texts. All included studies supported WGS surveillance on economic grounds, but the evidence was heterogeneous and limited for low- and middle-income country implementation and real-world effectiveness.
limitations: Does not justify a universal return-on-investment claim.

sourceLocator: knowledge-base/source-cards/microbial-genomics-implementation-collection.md
claim: Public-health bioinformatics implementation needs standardized, reproducible, validated, auditable, scalable, portable, and secure analysis within laboratory operating constraints.
extractedText: Source evidence note: Microbial Genomics implementation collection includes a bioinformatics paper on standardized, reproducible, validated, auditable, scalable, portable, and secure analysis.
limitations: Needs conversion into concrete checklists and examples.

sourceLocator: WHO 2023 national strategy support tool; WHO 2021 SARS-CoV-2 guide; ECDC 2016 expert opinion; PHE case study
claim: Genomic reports should be designed around the user, public-health question, timeframe, uncertainty and next decision rather than around analysis completion alone.
extractedText: WHO asks programmes to define objectives, users, action routes, turnaround and outcomes. The SARS-CoV-2 implementation guide calls for rapid, clear written reports, discussion of limitations, and stakeholder feedback. ECDC says reporting outputs should be agreed for each disease or pathogen according to surveillance objectives. PHE shows that enteric WGS reports serve health-protection, laboratory, food-safety, animal-health and international users.
notes: Use for the main reporting section and Gnomey profile-specific sharing.
limitations: Does not prescribe report wording or legal sign-off requirements.

sourceLocator: ECDC 2016 expert opinion; Armstrong et al. 2019; PHE case study; WHO 2021 SARS-CoV-2 guide
claim: The common reporting principle is stable across pathogens, but the practical content differs by organism and use case: enteric bacteria emphasise outbreak/source/One Health routes, TB emphasises transmission and drug resistance, respiratory viruses emphasise variants and vaccine or risk assessment, and AMR reporting depends on genotype-phenotype evidence.
extractedText: ECDC separates transmission-linkage and phenotype-prediction outputs. PHE describes enteric surveillance, outbreak, AMR and food or animal-health users. Armstrong et al. describe TB, influenza and AMR use cases and caveats. WHO's SARS-CoV-2 guide adds virus-specific stakeholder reporting, metadata, partial-genome and raw-read sharing considerations.
notes: Use for conditional organism-specific blocks after the main reporting section.
limitations: Specific organism thresholds and report templates still need current pathogen-specific sources.

sourceLocator: PHE case study; APHL 2016 implementation guide; WHO 2023 national strategy support tool
claim: A sample-to-report lifecycle should be operated as a monitored service loop with planned routes for routine throughput, failures, repeated analysis, reporting, sharing, feedback and later reuse.
extractedText: PHE describes routine scheduled throughput, LIMS-linked sequencing, storage, analysis, final reports, costs of sample issues or sequencing failure, troubleshooting, unexpected samples, validation and quality parameters. APHL describes NGS as multi-step and multi-day, recommends LIMS tracking across parallel conventional and NGS workflows, and links repository submissions to BioSample and SRA accession tracking. WHO adds timely feedback mechanisms linked to public-health action and national data-management protocols across storage, analysis, reporting and sharing.
notes: Use to make the lifecycle section more like a service model with exceptions and feedback, not a linear diagram.
limitations: Does not define a complete field dictionary, dashboard specification, or service-level agreement.

sourceLocator: WHO 2023 national strategy support tool; ECDC 2016 expert opinion; Gador-Whyte et al. 2026
claim: A programme should choose and document the service model before choosing infrastructure, because routine surveillance, outbreak response, reactive sequencing, prospective surveillance, repository submission, clinical support and research support have different requirements.
extractedText: WHO asks countries to define priority use cases, data users, sampling strategy, analysis, result sharing and outcomes, and says centralised or decentralised implementation depends on sample volume, capacity, transport, procurement and data-management protocols. ECDC states that storage and sharing requirements depend on surveillance objective and study design. The clinical review distinguishes reactive and prospective sequencing. PHE shows routine service planning around sample numbers, turnaround, user interaction, failure and reporting.
notes: Use for the practical service-model comparison in the whitepaper.
limitations: Does not by itself prescribe a single best service model.

sourceLocator: PHE case study, APHL 2016 implementation guide, and Gador-Whyte et al. 2026
claim: A routine genomics service needs a release and change-control route for workflows, databases, QC thresholds and reports, including validation evidence, version records, approval, rollback or emergency-fix criteria, and revalidation triggers.
extractedText: PHE validated before switching off conventional testing through parallel running, gap analysis and quality parameters. APHL describes validation runs, external review, final reports, SOP preparation, QA approval and sequencing quality metrics. The clinical implementation review requires database quality assessment, reproducible genotype-phenotype validation, strict software and database version control and workflow audit for reportable inferred phenotypes. WHO's platform guidance says integrated analytical tools should be documented and version-controlled.
notes: Use for beta-level change-control guidance without claiming to be an accreditation SOP.
limitations: Does not define jurisdiction-specific accreditation wording or organism-specific acceptance thresholds.

sourceLocator: WHO 2025 platform guidance, WHO 2021 SARS-CoV-2 implementation guide, ECDC 2016 expert opinion, and APHL 2016 implementation guide
claim: The beta metadata model should separate public minimum metadata, restricted contextual metadata, technical provenance, repository accession fields, and service-management fields, while preserving a common identifier lineage.
extractedText: WHO 2025 defines minimum metadata, optional metadata, provenance and identifiers across the sequence and metadata generation continuum. WHO 2021 gives date and location as absolute minimum SARS-CoV-2 metadata and encourages specimen type, sequencing method, duplicate flags and clinical or demographic data where confidentiality permits. ECDC separates non-confidential public metadata from sensitive clinical and epidemiological data. APHL shows how local services can track multiple isolate and repository identifiers, including SAMN and SRA IDs and dates for extraction, upload and database submission.
notes: Use to turn the metadata gap into a beta field model without pretending to have organism-specific schemas.
limitations: Organism-specific metadata fields, repository templates and legal requirements still need separate extraction and review.

sourceLocator: APHL 2016 implementation guide, PHE case study, WHO 2023 progress report, Gador-Whyte et al. 2026, and East African Community 2024 landscape assessment
claim: The beta guide can use a responsibility template that names required service functions and owners across laboratory, bioinformatics, data management, quality, IT, reporting, interpretation, user support and programme oversight, while leaving staffing ratios setting-specific.
extractedText: APHL, PHE, WHO, the clinical implementation review and the East African Community assessment all frame workforce as a multi-function service requirement. They support naming responsibilities across wet lab, bioinformatics, data handling, quality, reporting, interpretation, IT support, training and user engagement, but they do not provide universal staffing ratios.
notes: Use for a practical beta responsibility map in the workforce and costing section.
limitations: Does not define full-time equivalents, grades, salaries, or minimum staffing by throughput.

sourceLocator: PHA4GE implementation comparison, clinical implementation review, AusPathoGen national implementation account, and East African Community landscape assessment
claim: The beta guide should compare local, centralised, managed-platform, cloud, hybrid, collaborative and external-support models by dependencies, owners and failure modes rather than ranking one model as universally best.
extractedText: PHA4GE compares implementation examples across constraints such as internet, power, scalability, access control, provenance and administrative burden. The clinical implementation review names centralised, decentralised, hybrid and collaborative models. AusPathoGen provides a national collaborative example. The East African Community assessment shows why external sequencing or analysis should be treated as a managed dependency during capacity building.
notes: Use for the implementation-model dependency matrix and Gnomey constraint logic.
limitations: Does not provide numeric thresholds or a validated scoring instrument.

sourceLocator: WHO 2023 national strategy support tool, ECDC 2016 expert opinion, PHE case study, Armstrong et al. 2019, WHO 2021 SARS-CoV-2 guide, Gador-Whyte et al. 2026, and Microbial Genomics implementation collection
claim: The beta guide should use an organism and use-case service-model matrix to show how routine surveillance, outbreak response, AMR monitoring, repository submission, clinical support and research support change sampling, metadata, QC, reporting, sharing, validation and infrastructure needs.
extractedText: WHO places use-case definition at the start of strategy design. ECDC says outputs should be agreed for each disease or pathogen and separates transmission linkage from phenotype prediction. PHE, Armstrong et al., WHO SARS-CoV-2 guidance, the clinical implementation review and implementation examples show that enteric bacteria, respiratory viruses, TB, AMR and healthcare-associated infection services have different users, outputs and constraints.
notes: Use to close the beta service-model table gap without prescribing pathogen-specific thresholds.
limitations: Does not define organism-specific thresholds, exact report wording, repository fields or sampling fractions.

sourceLocator: Armstrong et al. 2019, ECDC 2016, Gador-Whyte et al. 2026, and Microbial Genomics implementation collection
claim: For TB, AMR and healthcare-associated infection support, beta reporting should separate relatedness or transmission inference, genotype-to-phenotype inference, clinical or infection-control action, validation basis, uncertainty and follow-up testing rather than merging them into one conclusion.
extractedText: Armstrong et al. and ECDC separate transmission inference from phenotype prediction and caution that genotype-phenotype inference depends on organism, drug, mechanism and evidence base. The clinical implementation review highlights reporting, interpretation, validation, EQA, regulatory obligations and uncertainty. Implementation examples show that AMR prediction, centre-specific typing and MDR outbreak genomics can influence public-health or infection-control decisions but require context and validation.
notes: Use for TB/AMR and healthcare-associated infection conditional reporting blocks.
limitations: Does not provide SNP thresholds, resistance-calling rules, clinical treatment recommendations, or jurisdiction-specific report wording.

sourceLocator: PHE case study, Allard et al. 2018, WHO 2021 SARS-CoV-2 guide, Armstrong et al. 2019, and Microbial Genomics implementation collection
claim: For enteric bacteria and respiratory viruses, beta reporting should make the public-health route explicit by separating sample and metadata context, genomic finding, quality limits, intended recipient, action boundary, sharing route and feedback or correction pathway.
extractedText: PHE and Allard et al. support enteric-bacterial reporting that connects clusters, source investigation, AMR or virulence findings, food/environmental links and multi-agency users. WHO SARS-CoV-2 guidance supports reporting respiratory-virus findings with sampling context, metadata, complete or partial genome status, sharing route, limitations, stakeholder communication and feedback. Armstrong et al. add that influenza sequence-first surveillance still needs selected phenotyping for some decisions.
notes: Use for enteric and respiratory-virus conditional reporting tables.
limitations: Does not provide detailed Salmonella, STEC, Listeria, influenza, RSV or SARS-CoV-2 report wording or thresholds.

sourceLocator: WHO 2022, WHO 2023, WHO 2025, ECDC 2016, WHO 2021 SARS-CoV-2 guide, and APHL 2016
claim: The beta guide can present data sharing as a decision table that separates purpose, route, data type, minimum metadata, sensitive metadata, quality status, access model, platform, timing, accession tracking, correction, withdrawal and local ownership.
extractedText: WHO, ECDC and APHL sources support a structured route map for data sharing: public-health purpose, national policy, platform attributes, public versus restricted metadata, data types, access model, repository or platform choice, BioSample and SRA accession tracking, correction routes, withdrawal routes and local responsibility. The source set supports a beta decision table but not jurisdiction-specific legal rules.
notes: Use to render the data-sharing pathway as an editable guidance table while retaining jurisdiction-specific caveats.
limitations: Does not prescribe repository-specific fields or legal/ethical decisions for a jurisdiction.

sourceLocator: WHO 2025, WHO 2021 SARS-CoV-2 guide, ECDC 2016, PHE case study, Armstrong et al. 2019, Gador-Whyte et al. 2026, and Microbial Genomics implementation collection
claim: The beta guide can give organism and use-case metadata priorities if it distinguishes them from exact repository schemas: enteric bacteria need exposure and source-context fields, respiratory viruses need sampling-frame and variant-context fields, TB and AMR need transmission and phenotype-inference context, and healthcare-associated infection needs ward, time, patient-care and infection-control context.
extractedText: WHO supports common metadata buckets and provenance. WHO SARS-CoV-2 guidance gives respiratory-virus minimum metadata and optional context. ECDC supports linkage to source metadata and public versus restricted metadata handling. PHE, Armstrong et al., the clinical review and implementation examples show organism-specific public-health uses that change which contextual fields matter most.
notes: Use for beta metadata table. Keep exact field names, controlled vocabularies and repository templates as a gap.
limitations: Does not define exact field names, mandatory status, controlled vocabularies, repository-required fields or jurisdictional permissions for sensitive fields.

sourceLocator: Egli and Howden 2026; Price et al. 2023; WHO 2024 costing tool manual; Khoo et al. 2025 national investment case framework
claim: Costing should distinguish continuous surveillance, selective surveillance, outbreak-triggered sequencing and pilot services because they create different recurring costs, detection benefits, workforce needs, response dependencies and evidence requirements.
extractedText: Egli and Howden contrast continuous or selective WGS surveillance with outbreak-triggered sequencing and frame the choice as a trade-off between steady surveillance costs and delayed detection risk. Price et al. show supportive but heterogeneous economic evidence. WHO's costing manual supports scenario-based costing categories, and Khoo et al. support explicit investment-case assumptions.
notes: Use for a strategy-choice table in the costing and investment sections. Keep numeric ROI claims out until full quantitative extraction is complete.
limitations: Does not justify a universal cost-per-sample, sampling fraction, return-on-investment estimate or recommended economic strategy.

sourceLocator: Stona et al. 2026; WHO 2023 progress report; WHO 2024 costing tool manual
claim: A sustainable pathogen genomics service should treat procurement, reagent resupply, equipment lead time, import procedures, maintenance, vendor support and transport as service-continuity risks, not only finance or purchasing details.
extractedText: Stona et al. identify high equipment and consumable costs, long equipment purchasing lead times, long reagent resupply times, customs clearance, variable import procedures, taxes and duties as supply-chain challenges for pathogen genomic surveillance. WHO progress reporting links supply chains to surveillance capacity, and WHO costing guidance includes reagents, consumables, equipment, maintenance, facilities, transport and bioinformatics categories.
notes: Use for a procurement-resilience checklist in workforce/sustainability/costing and implementation-model sections. Avoid prescribing stock levels or supplier choices.
limitations: Does not provide universal stock levels, lead times, supplier choices, customs procedures or procurement rules.

sourceLocator: de Jong et al. 2026; WHO 2022 global genomic surveillance strategy; WHO 2023 progress report
claim: The value case for genomic surveillance should include distributed capability and regular data flow, because early detection and global health security are weakened when capacity expands only in already high-capacity settings.
extractedText: de Jong et al. model respiratory-virus genomic surveillance and argue that geographically distributed minimum capacity with regular reporting improves early detection compared with siloed expansion in countries already possessing strong capacity. WHO strategy and progress sources frame access, geographic representation, workforce, data sharing, connectivity and readiness as linked genomic-surveillance capacities.
notes: Use in the why-genomics and investment-case sections. Avoid quoting the numeric threshold until the model assumptions are extracted.
limitations: Respiratory-virus modelling source; does not by itself define minimum capacity targets for all pathogens or countries.

sourceLocator: Gador-Whyte et al. 2026 clinical microbiology implementation review; Microbial Genomics implementation collection metagenomics examples
claim: The beta guide should make metagenomics a scope and service-model decision rather than silently treating metagenomic samples like isolate WGS, because metagenomics changes validation, QC, interpretation, reporting and clinical-governance requirements.
extractedText: The clinical microbiology review includes WGS and clinical metagenomics and highlights direct patient-care responsibilities, diagnostic reporting expectations, regulatory obligations, validation and uncertainty. Microbial Genomics examples show metagenomics can support food-source investigation and diagnostic or AMR-prediction edge cases, but the current source base does not support universal diagnostic rules.
notes: Use to add a scope-boundary row to the service-model matrix and keep metagenomic diagnostic guidance in gaps until source extraction is deeper.
limitations: Does not define metagenomic diagnostic workflows, thresholds, clinical decision rules or report wording.

sourceLocator: UKHSA 2024 strategy; Australian National Microbial Genomics Framework 2025-2027; AusPathoGen national implementation account
claim: A national pathogen genomics programme should be planned across capability domains, including public-health use cases, laboratory services, workforce, data and analytics, governance, collaboration, data sharing, quality, sustainability and evaluation.
extractedText: UKHSA, the Australian national framework and AusPathoGen all frame national pathogen genomics as more than sequencing capacity: they name laboratories, workforce, data and analytics, governance, collaboration, data sharing, standard methods, secure platforms, quality assurance, training, sustainability and evaluation.
notes: Use for a national-scale capability-domain table. Do not present as a certified maturity rubric.
limitations: Does not define universal governance structures, staffing ratios, national architecture, procurement rules or a validated maturity score.

sourceLocator: PHA4GE source pack rough disaster-recovery and power/cooling notes; PHE case study IT infrastructure sections; WHO 2023 national strategy support tool
claim: A programme using local servers or local storage should include physical infrastructure resilience in its storage and recovery plan, including controlled server-room access, environmental monitoring, cooling, UPS or power-failure planning, backup ownership, restore testing and escalation routes.
extractedText: PHA4GE rough source notes distinguish active storage, backup strategy and disaster recovery, and identify server-room access, environmental monitoring, cooling, UPS maintenance and generator support as local infrastructure concerns. PHE shows that operational WGS required high-performance and archive storage, data-lifecycle software, maintenance and enough storage and processing capacity for service delivery. WHO links storage, backup, retention, infrastructure, connectivity, local/cloud security and data criticality in national strategy planning.
notes: Use to add local infrastructure resilience checks for on-premises or hybrid storage. Keep formal facilities standards and numeric targets as gaps.
limitations: Does not define formal business-continuity policy, facilities standards, UPS sizing, generator capacity, RTO/RPO targets, retention periods or legal requirements.

sourceLocator: WHO 2023 national genomic surveillance strategy support tool; WHO 2024 genomics costing tool manual; economic value and costing synthesis note
claim: A pathogen genomics programme should turn costing categories into a costed implementation action register that names the activity, owner, implementation level, timeline, monitoring indicator, cost category, dependency and review status.
extractedText: WHO's national strategy support tool supports assigning prioritized activities to responsible entities, implementation level, timeline, monitoring indicators and cost. WHO's costing manual provides cost categories for throughput, platform, personnel, training, facilities, transport, bioinformatics, cloud or in-house server arrangements, storage, maintenance and quality management. Economic sources support explicit assumptions and transferability cautions rather than generic ROI claims.
notes: Use for a director/funder-facing action register that links cost categories to ownership and monitoring.
limitations: Does not provide local salary assumptions, procurement policies, numerical costs, cost-per-sample values, funding mechanisms or universal return-on-investment estimates.
